threats.title: Epoch Boundary Calculation Bug Enables Reward Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The update_market function incorrectly calculates the next epoch boundary as `i + BLOCK_EPOCH` instead of `epoch + BLOCK_EPOCH`. This allows an attacker to strategically time update_market calls to apply a higher reward rate from a previous epoch to blocks that actually belong to a lower-reward epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental logic error in the reward distribution math that directly violates the intended epoch-based emission schedule, allowing measurable extraction of excess CANTO.
preconditions: lastRewardBlock is not perfectly aligned with a BLOCK_EPOCH boundary

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: nextEpoch = i + BLOCK_EPOCH instead of (i / BLOCK_EPOCH + 1) * BLOCK_EPOCH
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker waits until mid-epoch to call update_market, dragging the previous epoch's rate into the current epoch's blocks
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures excess CANTO equal to the rate difference multiplied by the misallocated block delta

economic_questions: What is the maximum block delta that can be misallocated?
How much excess CANTO can be extracted per epoch transition?
validation_requirements: Simulate update_market across an epoch boundary where rate drops significantly
Calculate exact profit margins based on historical or planned cantoPerBlock values

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to the difference in cantoPerBlock and market share
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI (gas cost vs excess CANTO)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block (waiting for optimal epoch boundary timing)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after an epoch boundary where the previous epoch had a higher reward rate
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Drains protocol reward reserves faster than intended

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol treasury and other legitimate stakers
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Excess CANTO emitted beyond governance intent

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare i + BLOCK_EPOCH vs epoch + BLOCK_EPOCH

threats.title: Silent Downcast Truncation Destroys Reward Accumulator
threats.hypothesis_description: The explicit downcast to uint128 in update_market silently truncates values exceeding type(uint128).max in Solidity 0.8.x. An attacker can manipulate marketSupply to be extremely small (e.g., 1 wei), causing the accumulator to overflow uint128, silently truncate, and drastically reduce in value, destroying all pending rewards for existing users.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the fundamental monotonicity invariant of the reward accumulator. It allows an attacker to permanently destroy the yield of all other users in a market and potentially capture future rewards disproportionately.
preconditions: marketSupply can be reduced to a very small value (e.g., 1 wei) via withdrawals
cantoReward for the period is sufficient to cause (cantoReward * 1e18) / marketSupply to exceed ~3.4e38

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker withdraws almost all liquidity, leaving 1 wei, triggering a massive accumulator increase that truncates upon downcast
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker destroys competitors' pending rewards (cantoToSend underflows) and can redeposit to capture all future rewards

economic_questions: What is the exact cantoReward threshold required to overflow uint128 when marketSupply is 1 wei?
Can an attacker profitably execute this via flash loans or whale withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Test explicit downcast truncation behavior in Foundry
Simulate claim() underflow after accumulator truncation

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Sufficient capital to dominate market supply and withdraw it
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of future market rewards after wiping out existing debt
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High, depending on capital lockup costs

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires ability to withdraw majority of market supply
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When accrued rewards are high and market supply is concentrated
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete destruction of user yield for the affected market

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All existing depositors in the affected market
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending CANTO rewards for the market

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify silent truncation vs revert in Solidity 0.8.16

threats.title: Secondary Rewards Scaling Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: The calculation for secRewardsPerShare lacks decimal normalization (flagged by a 'TODO: Scaling' comment). If a whitelisted market uses a token with fewer than 18 decimals (e.g., USDC with 6 decimals), the secondary reward accumulator will be massively inflated, causing third-party protocols to distribute exponentially more rewards than intended.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a known incomplete implementation that directly impacts the economic integrity of any third-party protocol relying on the LendingLedger's secondary reward accounting.
deployment_assumptions: A whitelisted lending market uses a token with decimals other than 18 (e.g., USDC with 6 decimals)
preconditions: Third-party protocol uses secRewardDebt to distribute real economic value

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal deposits trigger massively inflated secRewardDebt due to denominator being 10^12 times smaller than expected
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker claims 10^12 times more secondary rewards from the integrated third-party protocol

economic_questions: Which third-party protocols integrate with secRewardDebt?
What is the exact inflation multiplier for a 6-decimal token?
validation_requirements: Deploy mock market with 6 decimals and measure secRewardsPerShare growth
Analyze third-party integration contracts for missing reverse-scaling

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal deposit in the affected market
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Total drain of the third-party protocol's secondary reward pool

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a 6-decimal market is whitelisted
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete drain of secondary reward reserves

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Third-party protocols integrated with Neofinance Coordinator
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire secondary reward pool of integrated protocols

threats.title: Rounding Truncation in Reward Debt Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: The rewardDebt calculation in sync_ledger uses truncating integer division. An attacker can split a large deposit into many tiny 'dust' deposits. On each deposit, the truncation discards a fraction of the rewardDebt increase, allowing the attacker to artificially minimize their rewardDebt and claim slightly more CANTO than entitled.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the profit per transaction is small, cheap gas on CANTO makes looping this attack potentially profitable, systematically draining protocol rewards.
preconditions: Gas costs are lower than the value of the truncated reward debt per transaction
Attacker can rapidly execute many sync_ledger calls via a smart contract

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker chooses _delta such that the product is just under 1e18, resulting in 0 rewardDebt increase
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains free user.amount without corresponding rewardDebt, leading to free CANTO claims

economic_questions: What is the maximum truncation loss per deposit?
Does the accumulated truncation profit exceed CANTO gas costs?
validation_requirements: Simulate a loop of 1000 deposits and measure net profit vs gas

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas cost for looped sync_ledger calls
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Marginal per tx, scales with loop count
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Low to Medium (highly dependent on gas price)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When gas prices are at their absolute minimum
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Slow, systematic drain of reward pool

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Gas cost vs truncation profit equation

threats.title: Missing Solvency Check Leads to Unbacked Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract does not verify that its native CANTO balance is sufficient to cover the rewards configured by governance via cantoPerBlock. If the contract is underfunded, early claimers will drain the balance, and subsequent claimers will experience permanent transaction reverts, locking their rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: This violates the core solvency invariant. Without enforcement, the protocol relies entirely on manual, off-chain treasury management, making insolvency a highly probable operational failure.
preconditions: Governance configures cantoPerBlock rates that exceed the contract's actual CANTO balance over time
Multiple users attempt to claim rewards

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No require(address(this).balance >= cantoToSend) before state update
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: First-come-first-serve run on the bank when users realize the contract is underfunded
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early claimers extract full value; late claimers get nothing (DoS)

economic_questions: Is there an automated funding mechanism, or is it purely manual?
What happens to user.rewardDebt if a claim reverts due to insufficient balance?
validation_requirements: Review deployment scripts for automated funding mechanisms
Simulate insolvency scenario and verify state preservation for late claimers

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Preservation of own capital at expense of others

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately upon realizing contract balance is dropping below pending claims
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Bank run causing panic and loss of trust

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of earned rewards if never funded

threats.title: Gas Limit DoS on Stale Markets
threats.hypothesis_description: The update_market function loops from lastRewardBlock to block.number in BLOCK_EPOCH chunks, making an external state-modifying call to gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write in every iteration. If a market is inactive for many epochs, the accumulated loop iterations will exceed the block gas limit, permanently bricking the market.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a structural unbounded loop vulnerability that can permanently freeze all user funds and rewards in a market simply due to the passage of time without activity.
preconditions: A whitelisted market has no sync_ledger or claim activity for a long period (many epochs)
The gas cost of the loop iterations exceeds the block gas limit

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Unbounded while loop with external state-modifying call
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker intentionally avoids updating a low-liquidity market until it bricks, or it happens naturally
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes permanent DoS and freezing of competitor funds

economic_questions: How many epochs does it take to hit the CANTO block gas limit?
Is there any way to incrementally advance lastRewardBlock?
validation_requirements: Calculate maximum safe epochs before DoS

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users with funds in the stale market
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of access to rewards and potentially deposits (if market relies on sync_ledger)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets tied to the stale market

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: CANTO block gas limit vs loop iteration gas cost

threats.title: Permanent Freeze of Rewards on Market De-whitelisting
threats.hypothesis_description: If governance de-whitelists a market, the update_market function immediately reverts for that market. Because claim() relies on update_market, users are permanently blocked from claiming rewards they have already earned. There is no grace period or emergency claim mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: While requiring admin action, this is a severe code defect in state handling. It traps user funds without recourse, turning a routine administrative action (deprecating a market) into a catastrophic loss event.
preconditions: Users have accrued but unclaimed CANTO rewards in that market

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Strict whitelist requirement blocks access to historical reward debt
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Governance action triggers immediate DoS on all user claims
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol treasury retains unpaid rewards at the expense of users

economic_questions: Is there any alternative path to extract rewardDebt?
Does re-whitelisting restore access without corrupting the accumulator?
validation_requirements: Simulate de-whitelisting and attempt to claim
Analyze state continuity if market is re-whitelisted later

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: None for attacker; 100% loss for victims

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Massive loss of user trust and frozen capital

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users of the de-whitelisted market
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending rewards for the market

threats.title: Arbitrary Balance Inflation by Compromised Market
threats.hypothesis_description: The sync_ledger function implicitly trusts the msg.sender (which must be a whitelisted market) to report accurate _lender addresses and _delta amounts. If a whitelisted market is compromised or turns malicious, it can report massive fabricated deposits for an attacker address, allowing the attacker to drain the entire CANTO reward pool.
threats.investigation_rationale: This highlights a critical trust boundary vulnerability. The protocol delegates all accounting authority to external contracts without any cross-verification of actual token balances, making the entire reward system as weak as the weakest whitelisted market.
preconditions: A whitelisted lending market is compromised via external exploit or malicious upgrade
The compromised market calls sync_ledger with a massive positive _delta for an attacker address

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Unverified state update based purely on msg.sender input
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised market inflates attacker's user.amount to 99.9% of total supply
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker claims nearly 100% of all future CANTO rewards allocated to that market, draining protocol funds

economic_questions: What is the security posture of the intended whitelisted markets?
Is there any upper bound on lendingMarketTotalBalance?
validation_requirements: Review architecture for potential cross-verification mechanisms
Simulate market compromise and subsequent reward drain

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Total drain of market's allocated CANTO rewards

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of protocol reward funds

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All CANTO allocated to the compromised market

threats.title: Retroactive Reward Rate Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The setRewards function allows governance to modify cantoPerBlock for any epoch, including past epochs. Because update_market is evaluated lazily, changing a past epoch's rate will retroactively alter the reward calculation for any market that has not been updated since that epoch, creating severe economic inconsistencies.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the immutability of historical economic state. It allows governance to retroactively steal earned rewards or unfairly inflate rewards for specific inactive markets.
preconditions: Governance calls setRewards for an epoch that has already passed
At least one market has a lastRewardBlock prior to the modified epoch

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Governance retroactively lowers rates to erase user debt, or raises rates to enrich insiders in a stale market
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Value is transferred arbitrarily between the protocol treasury and users based on retroactive state changes

economic_questions: Is there any legitimate operational reason to modify past epochs?
How much value can be retroactively altered based on typical update frequencies?
validation_requirements: Simulate retroactive rate reduction and measure impact on pending claims
Verify lack of historical immutability checks

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Variable, depends on the magnitude of the rate change

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When a market has been inactive for a long period
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of trust in protocol's economic guarantees

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending rewards in stale markets
